title: Microscopic theory of soft run-and-tumble particles
authors: Rosalba Garcia-Millan, Ziluo Zhang, Luca Cocconi, Marius Bothe, Letian Chen, Zigan Zhen, Gunnar Pruessner
abstract: Soft, repulsive run-and-tumble particles display emergent effective interactions as they appear to stick to each other in spite of the absence of attractive forces. This effective attraction emerges at strong enough repulsion and large self-propulsion. Complementing a companion paper that characterises effective attraction between two soft run-and-tumble particles [Garcia-Millan et al., Effective attraction by repulsion (2026)], here we provide a thorough derivation of our microscopic theory, which is an exact representation of the particle dynamics. We report the systematic calculation of the effective interaction vertices iteratively, in a perturbation expansion about the interaction couplings, by adding, order by order, loop corrections. We use the effective interaction vertices to calculate the two-point correlation function, fully characterising the stationary state. Other observables, such as the structure factor, overlap probability and entropy production rate are calculated as well.
summary: This paper provides a complete microscopic theory for the emergence of effective attraction in systems of repulsive, active particles. By using a systematic field-theoretic expansion, the authors show how self-propulsion and repulsion can lead to particle 'stickiness' and bound states. This work characterizes the resulting steady-state properties, including structure factors and entropy production.
main_problem: Developing a microscopic, analytical theory to explain how effective attraction (stickiness) emerges between soft, repulsive run-and-tumble particles (RTPs).
main_result: The authors provide an exact representation of particle dynamics and a systematic perturbation expansion that calculates effective interaction vertices and the stationary two-point correlation function, demonstrating that strong repulsion and high activity drive the transition from effective repulsion to attraction.
method: The study uses a field-theoretic approach (Doi-Peliti action) combined with a diagrammatic perturbation expansion and residue calculus to iteratively calculate interaction vertices and loop corrections.
model_or_system: A system of N interacting, soft, repulsive run-and-tumble particles on a one-dimensional periodic ring, modeled using overdamped Langevin equations with a Yukawa-type repulsive potential and Poissonian tumbling dynamics.
key_observables: Stationary two-point correlation function, static structure factor, compressibility factor, overlap probability, and entropy production rate.
important_parameters: Self-propulsion velocity (w), Péclet number (Pe), interaction coupling strength (nu), interaction length (xi), tumbling rate (gamma), and diffusion constant (Dx).
main_assumptions: The derivation is primarily focused on the two-particle (N=2) case; the interaction is limited to a Yukawa potential; the domain is periodic; and higher-order poles are neglected due to exponentially small amplitudes.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the iterative mechanism of pole generation; Figure 2 shows the transition of the structure factor from negative to positive values with increasing activity; Figure 3 and 4 show the emergence of bound states in correlation functions due to interaction strength and activity; Figure 5 shows overlap probability increases with activity; Figure 6 shows entropy production rate changes with repulsion and interaction length.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the Langevin dynamics and Fokker-Planck equation, moves to the derivation of the Doi-Peliti action, implements a Fourier-space representation, details an iterative scheme for calculating interaction vertices via loop corrections, and concludes with the calculation of steady-state observables and their dependence on activity and repulsion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

(43a) (red), and down-promotion with amplitude B, Eq. (43b) (black). Base case p(0) 1,1 = ξ−1, Eq. (39), is encircled in purple. Poles of ξ-type encircled on the left in orange are generated at every order through the final term in Eq. (42a) ∼L−1, which does not carry a factor of A or B. The poles labelled blue on the right are populated at every order from ai-poles in the square brackets in Eqs. (31), (33) and (35), which via Eq. (41) are shifted by Eq. (42) to ai ± ξ−1, so that p(i) n,−1 = ai −ξ−1 carries a...
FIG. 2. Structure factor Sj as a function of Fourier mode j for varying activity Pe = w2/(Dxγ). Parameters: Dx = 2, L = 20, ¯ν = 5, ¯ξ = 0.1, ¯γ = 0.02.

FIG. 3. Stationary two-point correlation functions P++ and P−+ Eqs. (55) as a function of the inter-particle distance x1 −x2, for increasing interaction coupling ¯ν. Parameters correspond to Fig. 2(a) in [1]: D = 0.5, L = 20, Pe = 20, ¯γ = 0.008, ¯ξ = 0.01.

FIG. 4. Stationary two-point correlation functions P++ and P−+ Eqs. (55) as a function of the inter-particle distance x1 −x2, for increasing activity Pe. Parameters correspond to Fig. 2(b) in [1]: D = 0.5, L = 20, ¯ν = 10, ¯γ = 0.008, ¯ξ = 0.01.

FIG. 5. Overlap probability pξ rescaled by its value in the non-interacting case, pξ = 2ξ/L, as a function of activity Pe for varying tumbling rate ¯γ. Symbols indicate the value of Pe at which the compressibility factor S1 changes sign from S1 < 0 (small Pe, effective repulsion) to S1 > 0 (large Pe, effective attraction). Parameters: Dx = 1, L = 20, ¯ν = 5, and ¯ξ = 0.01.
